title: engine.v1 — the shape of the engine manifest
description: The engine manifest is the one committed file that records what this repository's engine is made of: which engine release it runs, the version of each installed module, and the identity tier that governs how guardrail changes are gated. It is a lockfile-style inventory — the source of truth an upgrade reads to know each module's starting version — and doubles as the operator-readable record of the engine's contents. It lives at .engine/engine.json. This schema governs its shape only.

engine_release.description: The engine release this repository runs, as a version string (for example 1.2.0, or 0.0.0-dev while the engine is still being built). This is the single version an operator sees.

min_upgradeable_from.description: The oldest engine release proven to have a clean one-run upgrade path to this release, as a version string (for example 0.3.2). A deployed engine older than this cannot reconcile cleanly to this release — its own already-shipped upgrade code predates the reconcile — so the update refuses cleanly (nothing applied) and routes the operator to undo and stay on their current version, rather than opening a broken pull request. The value pattern is enforced so a malformed floor cannot silently disable the guard. Optional: absent on releases cut before this floor shipped, in which case no floor check runs.

packages.description: Each installed module mapped to its installed version (for example { "core": "1.2.0" }). These per-module versions are internal bookkeeping — the 'from' versions an upgrade reads before it overlays new code — not numbers the operator manages directly.

identity.description: The identity tier governing how the engine's own guardrails may change. solo: a single gate-holder — a guardrail can never weaken silently, but a weakening can still happen with the operator's explicit consent (the engine acts with the operator's credentials). team: a distinct engine identity holds enforcement-admin, so a guardrail cannot weaken at all. This is per-repository and re-chosen at first-run setup; it is not inherited from a template.

handle.description: The operator's own account handle, captured at first-run setup and preserved as operator config. It is the owner the engine writes into the code-ownership block for its own files, so an engine update re-renders that block for the new release's files while keeping this owner. Optional: it is absent until first-run setup derives and records it (and absent in the construction repository, which runs no first-run setup), so a manifest without it stays valid.

default_branch.description: The repository's default branch name, derived from GitHub/git at first-run setup and preserved as operator config. The engine reads it offline to tell whether the operator's top-level checkout is sitting on its main line or has drifted off it, so the check does not depend on a refs/remotes/origin/HEAD that is frequently unset. Optional: absent until first-run setup derives and records it (and absent in the construction repository, which runs no first-run setup), so a manifest without it stays valid.

home_repository.description: The engine's home repository as an owner/repo slug (for example StarshipSuperjam/engine-template) — the single coordinate that is the source of truth for where the engine fetches its own updates from, and for where a fork-native deployment escalates a contribution to. A generated repo is detached from its home (no upstream remote), so the engine updates by pulling that home's tagged GitHub releases, never by a git pull and never from this repo's own origin. Seeded from the template's committed manifest and carried forward at first-run setup; because it selects where executable engine code is fetched from, changing it to a different value is a guardrail-weakening change that needs the operator's explicit acknowledgment. Optional: absent in a repository generated before this coordinate shipped, in which case the engine cannot update and says so plainly rather than guessing a home.

product_repository.description: The repository this engine exists to build and work ON, as an owner/repo slug (for example StarshipSuperjam/engine-template) — the engine's PRODUCT, a DISTINCT coordinate from home_repository (which is where the engine's own code is fetched FROM). Recorded ONLY when the product is a repository DIFFERENT from the one the engine is deployed into (the fork-native / engine-mechanic arrangement of eADR-0026 'external-contribution-fork-native', where self = the deployed-into repo, product = the un-owned upstream it contributes to); when absent, the product IS this repository itself, derived live from the checkout's origin — so a self-building deployment stores nothing and cannot drift stale. INVARIANT (load-bearing): this is a display-only work-target LABEL. Nothing may fetch from, execute against, clone, or issue a GitHub write to this coordinate. A wrong value here can only mislabel a status line — never fetch or run code, which is exactly why (unlike home_repository) changing it is not a guardrail-weakening act. Giving it any consumer that fetches/executes/writes would inherit home_repository's supply-chain and command surface and is a re-classification event that must go back through the engine's guardrail-weakening analysis (eADR-0011) before it ships. Optional: absent on a self-building deployment and in a repository generated before this coordinate shipped.

control_plane.description: What the engine did to this repository's branch protection at setup, recorded so a later clean removal can reverse exactly that and nothing of the operator's. Written by the control-plane apply step on a brownfield arrival (and on greenfield, to record that the engine owns its own ruleset). Optional: absent until the gate is turned on, and on a repo whose protection was already fully in force.

ruleset_mode.description: created: the engine created and owns its own branch-protection ruleset (greenfield). augmented: the engine added its checks into a pre-existing PRODUCT ruleset in place (brownfield), so removal strips back only what it added and never deletes the operator's rule.

augmented_ruleset_id.description: The id of the product ruleset the engine augmented (when ruleset_mode is augmented); null when the engine created its own.

added.description: The exact pieces the engine added to a product ruleset, so removal reverses precisely them: the required-check contexts it unioned in, and any wholly-missing floor rule types it added. null when the engine created its own ruleset.

engine_identity.description: The distinct, non-admin GitHub identity that authors the engine's commits and pull requests in TEAM mode, so the operator becomes the enforced code-owner reviewer — a change cannot merge without a second sign-off, and this identity (holding no admin) cannot itself weaken the ruleset. Recorded when the operator switches solo->team via the team-switch operation; absent in solo (the default) and in the construction repository (which runs no first-run setup), so a manifest without it stays valid. Records only the PUBLIC identity — never a token or any secret, which stays in the operator's own credential store, so switching back to solo simply drops this record.

login.description: The machine-user account's GitHub login (for example 'my-project-engine') — a second account the operator created and added as a non-admin (write) collaborator, whose credential the operator's build sessions authenticate as. Commits and pull requests are attributed to it, and the code-owner review requirement is measured against it.

email.description: The commit-author email for that identity, set into git config so the engine's commits carry it. Optional: the login alone identifies the author; the email is the git-level attribution.
